În timp ce păcănelele pentru oameni încep să se închidă, oraşul a inaugurat recent prima sală de păcănele dedicată urşilor. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... medical.title: Researchers identify proteins fueling rare childhood leukemia, revealing new treatment strategy
medical.summary: Indiana University School of Medicine cancer researchers have identified a potential way to use existing, accessible drugs to combat juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia (JMML) by targeting a specific inflammatory pathway. The findings, published in the journal Blood, open the door to more effective treatment options for this rare and aggressive childhood blood cancer, especially for high-risk pediatric patients who currently face limited options.

medical.title: Global clinical trial reveals safest, most effective antibiotics for golden staph bloodstream infections
medical.summary: An international clinical trial has identified the optimal antibiotics for golden staph bloodstream infections, a breakthrough set to reshape treatment for the life-threatening condition. The SNAP Trial found that the standard antibiotic, flucloxacillin, should no longer be the drug of choice for treating the infection, revealing that cefazolin and benzylpenicillin offer safer and equally effective alternatives for patients.

medical.title: Cannabis caution as study finds increased psychosis risk for young people
medical.summary: Young people who use cannabis daily are up to four times more likely to develop psychosis than those who don't, according to an international study led by University of Queensland researchers. The review analyzed evidence from 17 previous studies to assess whether cannabis use played a contributory role in the development of mental health disorders, including psychosis, bipolar disorder, depression and anxiety.

medical.title: Microplastics may worsen fatty liver disease, new study suggests
medical.summary: Microplastics—minuscule pieces of plastic broken down from larger plastic waste—are a growing concern for human health, especially for the liver. A study from the University of Oklahoma, published in Science Advances, demonstrates that a common type of microplastic is particularly harmful to the liver under high-fat dietary conditions.

medical.title: Caregivers of children hospitalized for cancer, blood disorders at risk for food insecurity, researchers find
medical.summary: Researchers at Wake Forest University School of Medicine found caregivers of children hospitalized for cancer and blood disorders may experience food insecurity during their child's stay, even if they don't face that issue at home, and it could be linked to longer stays.

medical.title: Update to 89-year-old motor homunculus model shows brain's motor cortex isn't as neatly organized as previously thought
medical.summary: For almost a century, budding neuroscientists have been taught that the headband-like strip of brain tissue over our ears that controls our movements, called the motor cortex, contains an orderly map of our bodies. Brain cells concerned with moving each body part—from the tips of our toes to the tips of our fingers—are all laid out in sequence, as well as a large zone dedicated to our fabulously expressive faces and speech muscles. That's the textbook account, anyway.

medical.title: Most Americans are surviving cancer, but the mental health challenges can persist
medical.summary: An increasing number of Americans are getting—and surviving—cancer. There were more than 18 million cancer survivors in the U.S. in 2025, and the National Cancer Institute estimates that number will grow to 22 million by 2035. But long after completing treatment, many survivors face lingering mental health challenges that go unaddressed.

medical.title: Study finds variety, intensity of exercise lower odds of depressive symptoms
medical.summary: Engaging in a variety of physical activities—especially those of higher intensity—may be linked to lower odds of experiencing depressive symptoms, according to new research published in Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise.

medical.title: Better heart 'digital twins' could help target treatment for atrial fibrillation
medical.summary: A cross-university paper led by researchers at Queen Mary University of London, published in the Journal of Physiology, shows how better "digital twins" could help doctors treat people with atrial fibrillation.

medical.title: Before tangles kill neurons, tau-linked transport defects may be reversible
medical.summary: Neurons, specialized cells that transmit information across the nervous system, communicate with each other via projections known as axons. These microscopic, cable-like structures are also used to deliver proteins, signaling molecules and other cargo across different areas of the brain.

medical.title: Colonoscopy remains the top choice for colorectal cancer screening amid new guidance
medical.summary: As new colorectal cancer screening guidance draws attention to the growing number of testing options, the American Gastroenterological Association urges patients and primary care providers to understand how those options compare. AGA experts emphasize that screening saves lives—and that the type of test matters.

medical.title: What went right this week: a ‘turning point’ for mangroves, plus more
medical.summary: A ‘global turning point for mangroves’, a new France-sized marine reserve, and how EVs save lives, plus more The post What went right this week: a ‘turning point’ for mangroves, plus more appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Dementia risk linked to nitrate in drinking water, study finds
medical.summary: A major long-term study of more than 54,000 adults found that where nitrate comes from may matter far more than how much you consume. People who got more nitrate from vegetables—roughly the amount in a cup of baby spinach a day—had a lower risk of developing dementia, while higher nitrate and nitrite intake from red meat, processed meat, and even drinking water was linked to a greater risk.

science.title: Orangutans eat medicinal plants in patterns that suggest self-medication
science.summary: Orangutans seek out plants with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory and wound-healing properties, new research shows. Based on 20 years of observations of orangutans in Indonesian Borneo, scientists assessed how often the animals ate plants with known medicinal benefits. The findings, published in Scientific Reports, suggest orangutans eat combinations of plants in specific sequences—consistent with "self-medication" seen in other species.

science.title: Q&A: Do high-pressure environments encourage moral disengagement?
science.summary: Why do individuals and organizations sometimes rationalize decisions that conflict with their stated values? According to Leah P. Hollis, professor of education policy studies in the Penn State College of Education, the answer may lie in "moral disengagement"—a psychological process through which people justify actions that might otherwise conflict with ethical or institutional standards.

science.title: Study yields new insights on what makes conversation engaging
science.summary: What makes a speaker engaging? Both what is said and how it is said matter, but in different, complementary ways, a new study conducted at the McGill School of Communication Sciences and Disorders has found.

science.title: Pigeons' eyes are almost perfectly still when they're flying, study finds
science.summary: When pigeons are flying through the air, they lock their eyes in a near-fixed position instead of moving them around. Researchers from Harvard University discovered this after attaching a lightweight rig of cameras and mirrors to nine pigeons before they took off on short flights. Their findings are published in a paper in the journal Current Biology.

science.title: Famous 'Pink Planet' harbors a salty surprise
science.summary: Northwestern University-led astronomers have discovered salty skies surrounding the universe's famous "Pink Planet." For more than a decade, the ancient, rosy-hazed world kept astronomers guessing. One of the coldest known planetary-mass companions ever directly imaged, the elusive object is too faint for astronomers to dissect its light from Earth. But new observations from the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) reveal an atmosphere filled with exotic chemistry—and salty clouds unlike anything seen before.

science.title: Screens dominate the dinner table, with 77.6% of parents using devices
science.summary: Food has always had a unique way of bringing people together. It becomes especially evident during family mealtimes, when children and adults gather around the table to share more than just a meal. They share stories, catch up on their day and discuss issues they are dealing with. The scene at the dinner table has, however, changed. People still sit together, but attention often shifts away from the conversation to the devices in their hands.

science.title: Beyond bread and beer, alluring yeast species could yield new mosquito traps to combat malaria
science.summary: An orange-colored yeast species isolated from a Baltimore sidewalk several years ago could be the basis of eco-friendly mosquito traps that reduce malaria transmission, according to a new study from researchers at the Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health.

science.title: Ripple-like rings of the 'Bullseye galaxy' could be explained by dark matter
science.summary: Two U.S. physicists have suggested that the nine concentric rings surrounding the galaxy LEDA 1313424, also known as the Bullseye galaxy, could have emerged through the quantum behavior of particles of dark matter. Through analysis published in The Astrophysical Journal, Pierre Sikivie and Yuxin Zhao at the University of Florida argue that the extraordinary structure wasn't created by a collision between galaxies, as previous theories had suggested—but by a Bose-Einstein condensate of axions.

science.title: Groundwater flow could help unlock ocean carbon storage solution
science.summary: AIMS scientists are quantifying the amount of groundwater flowing from the land to the Ningaloo Reef in Western Australia to help understand the viability of blue carbon storage solutions to reduce the impacts of climate change.

science.title: Hubble Glimpses Merging Galaxy Clusters
science.summary: This NASA Hubble Space Telescope image features a galaxy cluster, called CL0016+1609 or MACS J0018.5+1626, that is very bright at X-ray wavelengths and is one of the most extensively studied clusters at X-ray and radio wavelengths. The X-ray observations of this cluster revealed that it is two clusters merging along our line of sight. Researchers […]

science.title: What is space-time? A mystery at the heart of reality
science.summary: What if our biggest idea about reality is built on a hidden misunderstanding? A new philosophical look at space-time challenges the popular view that the past, present, and future all exist together in a timeless "block universe." The argument suggests that physicists may be blurring the difference between things that exist and things that merely occur, creating deep confusion about what space-time actually is.

science.title: Scientists may have debunked one of humanity's oldest habits
science.summary: Ancient grooves on human teeth, once hailed as evidence of tooth-picking, may simply be the result of natural wear, according to a new study of wild primates. The research also revealed that a common modern dental defect appears to be uniquely human, hinting that today's lifestyles may be reshaping our teeth in unexpected ways.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.summary: Inaugurată cu mare fast, biserica de 20 de milioane de euro a lui Gig Becali nu a rezistat foarte mult. Contrar directivelor date de latifundiarul din Pipera, în biserica recent inaugurată a intrat o femeie, iar inevitabilul s-a produs: biserica s-a prăbușit. „Pe de-o parte sunt supărat, că s-a prăbușit biserica, dar pe de alta […] The post Previzibil! Biserica lui Gigi Becali s-a prăbuşit după ce a intrat o femeie în ea appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 Ca să nu mai stea urşii pe străzi, în Braşov s-a deschis o sală de păcănele la care câştigi miere
mix_items.summary: Exasperaţi că străzile sunt pline de blănoşi nepoftiţi, majoritatea urşi, braşovenii au decis să ia măsuri! În timp ce păcănelele pentru oameni încep să se închidă, oraşul a inaugurat recent prima sală de păcănele dedicată urşilor. Acum, animalele au unde să se adune noaptea, nu mai răscolesc prin gunoaie şi nu îi mai deranjează pe […] The post Ca să nu mai stea urşii pe străzi, în Braşov s-a deschis o sală de păcănele la care câştigi miere appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Orangutans eat medicinal plants in patterns that suggest self-medication
mix_items.summary: Orangutans seek out plants with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory and wound-healing properties, new research shows. Based on 20 years of observations of orangutans in Indonesian Borneo, scientists assessed how often the animals ate plants with known medicinal benefits. The findings, published in Scientific Reports, suggest orangutans eat combinations of plants in specific sequences—consistent with "self-medication" seen in other species.

mix_items.title: Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor
mix_items.summary: Alexandra, adolescentă sprijinită de Fundația Inocenți, și-a recăpătat încrederea în sine și motivația pentru învățare prin suport educațional și emoțional. The post Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Q&A: Do high-pressure environments encourage moral disengagement?
mix_items.summary: Why do individuals and organizations sometimes rationalize decisions that conflict with their stated values? According to Leah P. Hollis, professor of education policy studies in the Penn State College of Education, the answer may lie in "moral disengagement"—a psychological process through which people justify actions that might otherwise conflict with ethical or institutional standards.

mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... mix_items.title: Researchers identify proteins fueling rare childhood leukemia, revealing new treatment strategy
mix_items.summary: Indiana University School of Medicine cancer researchers have identified a potential way to use existing, accessible drugs to combat juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia (JMML) by targeting a specific inflammatory pathway. The findings, published in the journal Blood, open the door to more effective treatment options for this rare and aggressive childhood blood cancer, especially for high-risk pediatric patients who currently face limited options.

mix_items.title: Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat
mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Study yields new insights on what makes conversation engaging
mix_items.summary: What makes a speaker engaging? Both what is said and how it is said matter, but in different, complementary ways, a new study conducted at the McGill School of Communication Sciences and Disorders has found.

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Pigeons' eyes are almost perfectly still when they're flying, study finds
mix_items.summary: When pigeons are flying through the air, they lock their eyes in a near-fixed position instead of moving them around. Researchers from Harvard University discovered this after attaching a lightweight rig of cameras and mirrors to nine pigeons before they took off on short flights. Their findings are published in a paper in the journal Current Biology.

mix_items.title: Global clinical trial reveals safest, most effective antibiotics for golden staph bloodstream infections
mix_items.summary: An international clinical trial has identified the optimal antibiotics for golden staph bloodstream infections, a breakthrough set to reshape treatment for the life-threatening condition. The SNAP Trial found that the standard antibiotic, flucloxacillin, should no longer be the drug of choice for treating the infection, revealing that cefazolin and benzylpenicillin offer safer and equally effective alternatives for patients.

mix_items.title: Famous 'Pink Planet' harbors a salty surprise
mix_items.summary: Northwestern University-led astronomers have discovered salty skies surrounding the universe's famous "Pink Planet." For more than a decade, the ancient, rosy-hazed world kept astronomers guessing. One of the coldest known planetary-mass companions ever directly imaged, the elusive object is too faint for astronomers to dissect its light from Earth. But new observations from the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) reveal an atmosphere filled with exotic chemistry—and salty clouds unlike anything seen before.

mix_items.title: Cannabis caution as study finds increased psychosis risk for young people
mix_items.summary: Young people who use cannabis daily are up to four times more likely to develop psychosis than those who don't, according to an international study led by University of Queensland researchers. The review analyzed evidence from 17 previous studies to assess whether cannabis use played a contributory role in the development of mental health disorders, including psychosis, bipolar disorder, depression and anxiety.

mix_items.title: Microplastics may worsen fatty liver disease, new study suggests
mix_items.summary: Microplastics—minuscule pieces of plastic broken down from larger plastic waste—are a growing concern for human health, especially for the liver. A study from the University of Oklahoma, published in Science Advances, demonstrates that a common type of microplastic is particularly harmful to the liver under high-fat dietary conditions.

mix_items.title: Screens dominate the dinner table, with 77.6% of parents using devices
mix_items.summary: Food has always had a unique way of bringing people together. It becomes especially evident during family mealtimes, when children and adults gather around the table to share more than just a meal. They share stories, catch up on their day and discuss issues they are dealing with. The scene at the dinner table has, however, changed. People still sit together, but attention often shifts away from the conversation to the devices in their hands.

mix_items.title: Caregivers of children hospitalized for cancer, blood disorders at risk for food insecurity, researchers find
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Wake Forest University School of Medicine found caregivers of children hospitalized for cancer and blood disorders may experience food insecurity during their child's stay, even if they don't face that issue at home, and it could be linked to longer stays.

mix_items.title: Update to 89-year-old motor homunculus model shows brain's motor cortex isn't as neatly organized as previously thought
mix_items.summary: For almost a century, budding neuroscientists have been taught that the headband-like strip of brain tissue over our ears that controls our movements, called the motor cortex, contains an orderly map of our bodies. Brain cells concerned with moving each body part—from the tips of our toes to the tips of our fingers—are all laid out in sequence, as well as a large zone dedicated to our fabulously expressive faces and speech muscles. That's the textbook account, anyway.

mix_items.title: Beyond bread and beer, alluring yeast species could yield new mosquito traps to combat malaria
mix_items.summary: An orange-colored yeast species isolated from a Baltimore sidewalk several years ago could be the basis of eco-friendly mosquito traps that reduce malaria transmission, according to a new study from researchers at the Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health.

mix_items.title: Most Americans are surviving cancer, but the mental health challenges can persist
mix_items.summary: An increasing number of Americans are getting—and surviving—cancer. There were more than 18 million cancer survivors in the U.S. in 2025, and the National Cancer Institute estimates that number will grow to 22 million by 2035. But long after completing treatment, many survivors face lingering mental health challenges that go unaddressed.

mix_items.title: Study finds variety, intensity of exercise lower odds of depressive symptoms
mix_items.summary: Engaging in a variety of physical activities—especially those of higher intensity—may be linked to lower odds of experiencing depressive symptoms, according to new research published in Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise.

mix_items.title: Ripple-like rings of the 'Bullseye galaxy' could be explained by dark matter
mix_items.summary: Two U.S. physicists have suggested that the nine concentric rings surrounding the galaxy LEDA 1313424, also known as the Bullseye galaxy, could have emerged through the quantum behavior of particles of dark matter. Through analysis published in The Astrophysical Journal, Pierre Sikivie and Yuxin Zhao at the University of Florida argue that the extraordinary structure wasn't created by a collision between galaxies, as previous theories had suggested—but by a Bose-Einstein condensate of axions.

mix_items.title: Better heart 'digital twins' could help target treatment for atrial fibrillation
mix_items.summary: A cross-university paper led by researchers at Queen Mary University of London, published in the Journal of Physiology, shows how better "digital twins" could help doctors treat people with atrial fibrillation.

mix_items.title: Before tangles kill neurons, tau-linked transport defects may be reversible
mix_items.summary: Neurons, specialized cells that transmit information across the nervous system, communicate with each other via projections known as axons. These microscopic, cable-like structures are also used to deliver proteins, signaling molecules and other cargo across different areas of the brain.

mix_items.title: Colonoscopy remains the top choice for colorectal cancer screening amid new guidance
mix_items.summary: As new colorectal cancer screening guidance draws attention to the growing number of testing options, the American Gastroenterological Association urges patients and primary care providers to understand how those options compare. AGA experts emphasize that screening saves lives—and that the type of test matters.

mix_items.title: Groundwater flow could help unlock ocean carbon storage solution
mix_items.summary: AIMS scientists are quantifying the amount of groundwater flowing from the land to the Ningaloo Reef in Western Australia to help understand the viability of blue carbon storage solutions to reduce the impacts of climate change.

mix_items.title: Hubble Glimpses Merging Galaxy Clusters
mix_items.summary: This NASA Hubble Space Telescope image features a galaxy cluster, called CL0016+1609 or MACS J0018.5+1626, that is very bright at X-ray wavelengths and is one of the most extensively studied clusters at X-ray and radio wavelengths. The X-ray observations of this cluster revealed that it is two clusters merging along our line of sight. Researchers […]

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: What went right this week: a ‘turning point’ for mangroves, plus more
mix_items.summary: A ‘global turning point for mangroves’, a new France-sized marine reserve, and how EVs save lives, plus more The post What went right this week: a ‘turning point’ for mangroves, plus more appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: What is space-time? A mystery at the heart of reality
mix_items.summary: What if our biggest idea about reality is built on a hidden misunderstanding? A new philosophical look at space-time challenges the popular view that the past, present, and future all exist together in a timeless "block universe." The argument suggests that physicists may be blurring the difference between things that exist and things that merely occur, creating deep confusion about what space-time actually is.

mix_items.title: Scientists may have debunked one of humanity's oldest habits
mix_items.summary: Ancient grooves on human teeth, once hailed as evidence of tooth-picking, may simply be the result of natural wear, according to a new study of wild primates. The research also revealed that a common modern dental defect appears to be uniquely human, hinting that today's lifestyles may be reshaping our teeth in unexpected ways.

mix_items.title: Dementia risk linked to nitrate in drinking water, study finds
mix_items.summary: A major long-term study of more than 54,000 adults found that where nitrate comes from may matter far more than how much you consume. People who got more nitrate from vegetables—roughly the amount in a cup of baby spinach a day—had a lower risk of developing dementia, while higher nitrate and nitrite intake from red meat, processed meat, and even drinking water was linked to a greater risk.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Ce plan ai pe azi? - Sa nu stric planul de ieri.

satire_ro.title: Răfuială cu săbii între clanurile Mega Image şi Froo pentru un parter de bloc din Bucureşti
